entries.decision: Discoveries are recorded in-repo as discoveries/*.json (not GitHub issues).
entries.rationale: Matches lume's 'state as JSON in the repo' philosophy, is contract-testable, and adds no external coupling. One schema, one scan, two sources (lume's own operator feedback and adopter repos).

entries.decision: Autonomous acceptance for the consumer (tredl) is STRICT: auto-accept only when the Definition of Done is machine-verifiable; anything subjective escalates to the human. No confidence thresholds in v0.1.
entries.rationale: Confidence thresholds are how autonomous agents ship confident garbage. tredl-as-operator only has value if its acceptance is independent and verifiable - this is why L2 (DoD machine-evaluable, read at accept) is the keystone.

entries.decision: lume owns the MECHANISM to instantiate a workstream/phase structure from a declarative pattern definition; the consumer (tredl) owns the INTELLIGENCE of choosing/authoring a pattern. Built-in named templates are content grown by demonstrated need only, never speculatively.
entries.rationale: Keeps lume's focus on deterministic mechanism and pushes goal-fitting judgement to the consumer; a speculative template library is the scope-creep trap. This is why L5 is deferred and discovery-triggered, not scheduled.

entries.context: accept-gate friction surfaced driving iteration 003 of this workstream
entries.decision: Prioritise P3/L2 (machine-evaluable DoD read at accept) as the next execution work; the keystone, not just plan-order.
entries.rationale: The accept gate today is a pure human assertion - L0.2 confirmed no verb reads the DoD. That is exactly why the gate adds friction: an in-session agent cannot relay the operator's 'approve' without appearing to self-accept, so every accept forces a manual operator command. Building P3 first makes accept a verifiable check (tests/lint/schema green = accept; subjective = human escalation), which dissolves the friction in a principled way rather than by loosening the human-in-the-loop rule. This is also tredl's whole premise (autonomy bounded by verifiability), so it must land before any auto-accept path.

entries.decision: DoD checks hook into Workstream.transition() on verb=='accept', before the phase mutation, raising GateError on any failed check.
entries.rationale: transition() is the single function that gates accept (handback->accepted) and already raises GateError mapped to exit 1 by handle_transition; evaluating there reuses the existing refuse path with no CLI/handler change. It is also the only place that currently never reads the DoD - the exact gap Premise A names.

entries.decision: Each DoD item gains an OPTIONAL 'check' object; v0.1 supports exactly three predicate kinds: command (exit 0), file-exists, schema-valid (reusing validate_entity).
entries.rationale: Optional keeps iteration_content backward-compatible (existing prose-only items still validate; no migration). The three kinds are deterministic and already expressible with lume's own seams (subprocess, path, validate). Arbitrary predicate languages / output-regex / HTTP probes are deferred as scope-creep traps.

entries.decision: Split veto from autonomy: lume REFUSES accept if any verifiable check fails and EXPOSES verifiability status, but never auto-accepts; the auto-accept-vs-escalate decision lives in the consumer (tredl).
entries.rationale: Preserves lume's operator-in-the-loop contract (lume only gains a machine VETO, not the power to accept) while giving an autonomous operator the signal it needs: fully-verifiable + all-green may auto-accept; any prose-only item escalates to a human. Matches iteration 001 decision #2 (STRICT autonomous acceptance) and keeps the intelligence in tredl per the template split.

entries.decision: Build a pure evaluate_dod(content_doc, repo_root)->results function; accept calls it for the veto AND ship a 'lume check' verb in v0.1 that calls it for a dry-run (pass/fail without transitioning); verifiability status is surfaced in 'lume check' output and as a field on 'lume get'.
entries.rationale: A pure evaluator is unit-testable in isolation and reused by both accept and the dry-run. 'lume check' is cheap and high-value: it lets a human preview the gate and lets tredl read pass/fail + verifiable/prose-only status before deciding accept-vs-escalate, without a state transition.

entries.decision: command checks run with cwd=repo root, a default 120s timeout, inheriting the current env; trust model = same as running the test suite (DoD author == operator). Sandbox/allowlist is OUT of v0.1 and recorded as a discovery-triggered follow-up; 'lume check'/status flags that a DoD contains command checks.
entries.rationale: Matches how the project already runs (pytest from repo root, no sandbox). Flagging command-bearing DoDs gives an autonomous operator the signal to apply its own trust policy. A real sandbox is non-trivial and must be demand-driven, not speculative - but the hole is named, not hidden.

entries.decision: v0.1 RECOMPUTES checks at each accept (and on 'lume check'); no separate persisted check-result artifact. The accept verdict's reason carries a one-line summary of what was evaluated for an audit trail.
entries.rationale: Checks are cheap and a pure re-runnable evaluator avoids staleness/cache-invalidation questions. Persisting a results artifact is speculative complexity; the verdict summary gives enough audit without a new schema.

entries.decision: On any failed/failed-to-run check, accept raises GateError (exit 1, phase stays handback) with message 'cannot accept - N DoD check(s) failed' followed by per-item 'text -> reason'; --json error payload carries a structured 'failures' list (item index, text, kind, reason).
entries.rationale: Reuses the existing GateError->exit-1 path so no handler change; the structured --json failures list is what an autonomous operator (tredl) parses to report or escalate, while the text message serves a human.

entries.decision: The accept veto refuses with a clear multi-line GateError message (each failed item -> reason); the STRUCTURED machine-readable failures list is delivered by P9's 'lume check', not by extending the shared accept error plumbing.
entries.rationale: Routing structured results to 'lume check' is better design than overloading the {error:{code,message}} shape used by every verb: an autonomous operator runs 'lume check' to read per-item pass/fail BEFORE attempting accept, and the human-facing accept error stays a clear message. Keeps P8 to the veto and avoids touching shared error plumbing for marginal gain.

entries.decision: Keep plugin.json as the SINGLE version source and make the pinnable release artifact a git tag equal to that version; do NOT add a pyproject.toml merely to satisfy P5's packaging clause.
entries.rationale: lume ships as a Claude Code marketplace plugin (source ./plugin), stdlib-only, never pip-installed. A pyproject would declare a package nobody installs and duplicate the version, risking drift. For a git-repo marketplace the pin IS a tag/ref, so a semver tag == plugin.json version is the real packaging for this channel. P5 DONE-WHEN clause 2 is refined accordingly (recorded in discovery, not silently changed).

entries.decision: CI = a GitHub Actions workflow running 'python -m pytest -q' on push and pull_request, matrix python 3.10/3.11/3.12/3.13; making it a REQUIRED status check is the operator's branch-protection setting, not an agent action.
entries.rationale: future-annotations is in all 25 modules and there are no match statements, so the syntax floor is low; 3.10 is a safe, widely-available minimum and the matrix proves the upper range. Required-check enforcement belongs to the human per lume's gate rule.

entries.context: P5/L4 planning (iter 011) PYPROJECT + FORK B - version
entries.decision: Add pyproject.toml (operator decision) for package metadata + dev/tooling over the src-layout (plugin/src/lume); plugin.json stays the single version of record; a consistency check asserts pyproject.version == plugin.json.version and the release tag == that version, failing CI on drift.
entries.rationale: Operator wants a pyproject; the risk it introduces is a second version source, so the mitigation is a hard consistency check rather than trusting humans to sync three places. PyPI is not made the primary channel - the marketplace plugin remains the install path.

entries.decision: Release = a git tag vX.Y.Z (== plugin.json version) plus a GitHub release; the tag/ref IS the pinnable artifact for the marketplace plugin; attaching a built archive is optional and not required for v0.1.
entries.rationale: For a git-repo marketplace, an adopter pins by tag/ref, so the tag is the deliverable; a built archive adds little for a stdlib-only directory-source plugin. Keep the release flow minimal.

entries.context: P5/L4 planning (iter 011) FORK D - install verification
entries.decision: Close the [U] live-install caveat with a documented, reproduced manual verification (steps from a clean state via '/plugin marketplace add' + '/plugin install'), recorded in the repo; an automated live-install CI job is OUT of v0.1 because it needs the Claude CLI in CI.
entries.rationale: test_smoke_install already covers the entry point automatically; the residual gap is the live Claude Code path, which cannot be automated without the Claude CLI in CI. A documented human-reproduced verification is the honest v0.1 close; automating it is a later option, not a v0.1 blocker.

entries.decision: Python floor is 3.11 (operator-approved), so the version-consistency check uses stdlib tomllib with no third-party dependency; CI matrix becomes 3.11-3.13.
entries.rationale: Reading pyproject's version to compare against plugin.json needs a TOML parser; tomllib is stdlib from 3.11. Raising the floor from the proposed 3.10 to 3.11 avoids a tomli dependency or a vendored parser, keeping lume dependency-free.
